comments:
Summary
This paper presents an integrated approach to semantic dependency parsing / semantic role labeling (SRL) that unifies predicate identification/disambiguation and argument identification/classification into a single word-pair classification framework. Key technical contributions are (1) adaptive argument candidate pruning with auxiliary “noMore…” labels that drastically reduces candidate sets while losing few true arguments, (2) large-scale feature template generation (781 templates) and a practical greedy feature-template selection algorithm (detailed as Algorithm 1), and (3) a decoding procedure based on a conditional Maximum Entropy model with beam search that incorporates global features. The system is evaluated on CoNLL-2008 corpora with several syntactic inputs (gold, MST parser, and Johansson & Nugues parser) and compared against top systems; results indicate competitive Sem-F1 scores close to top pipeline systems and better than most joint approaches reported at the time.

Strengths
- The paper addresses a meaningful problem: integrating SRL subtasks into a single model while retaining high performance and practical efficiency.
- The adaptive pruning idea is simple, linguistically motivated, and shown to give large runtime savings with minimal loss in coverage; empirical results quantify training and testing time reductions and coverage trade-offs.
- The feature engineering is thorough: the authors systematically decompose feature elements, generate a large template space, and provide a practical, empirically efficient greedy selection algorithm to obtain smaller high-performing template sets. The analysis of resulting template sets (differences between syntactic-path and linear-path selections, and between NomBank/PropBank) is informative.
- Experiments are comprehensive: multiple syntactic inputs, ablation (sense-features, syn vs lin paths), and comparisons to several top systems are reported. The evaluation metrics are standard and well presented.
- The system is practical and close in performance to the best pipeline systems while maintaining integrative design; the paper discusses trade-offs (e.g., reranking/joint methods give small gains at high cost).

Weaknesses and Suggestions
- Not fully joint/synchronous: The model is integrative in representation and features, but the predicate and argument recognition are still done sequentially (predicates first, then arguments). The paper acknowledges this; however, more explicit discussion of the limitations this imposes (e.g., error propagation from predicate decisions) and empirical quantification would strengthen the contribution.
- Beam search vs exact inference: Beam search is used for decoding (an approximate greedy search). More details about beam size, sensitivity to beam width, and runtime-memory trade-offs in decoding would be valuable. Also, an empirical comparison versus an exact inference method (ILP) on a small subset could help clarify the impact of approximation.
- Reproducibility details: while the paper states that feature templates are available, it would help to list exact hyperparameters used for ME (regularization sigma), beam size, optimizer settings, and training time/memory on representative hardware so others can reproduce results.
- Error analysis: the paper gives overall Sem-F1 and many comparisons, but more qualitative/error-type analyses (which roles or constructions fail, where nominal vs verbal predicates differ, or examples of mistakes introduced by pruning) would be useful to guide future improvements.
- Interaction with syntactic quality: the paper measures Sem-F1/LAS ratios and notes tolerance to imperfect syntax, but a more detailed analysis of which syntactic errors hurt semantic decisions would be beneficial.
- Feature selection algorithm: the empirical complexity analysis is convincing, but more explanation of how the randomized initial S affects reproducibility and final sets would be useful; perhaps report variance across different random starts.

Minor points / Clarifications
- Clarify precisely how predicate senses are modeled: the paper says senses are always combined with word forms, which helps precision — giving a concrete example in the paper text would help readers.
- Provide the beam width used in experiments and whether the beam is applied per-predicate or globally.
- Provide a short table of runtime (train/test) and memory usage for the main reported settings.

Overall Assessment and Recommendation
This paper presents valuable engineering and empirical advances for SRL: pragmatic, well-motivated pruning and a scalable, effective feature-selection pipeline that together yield a competitive integrative SRL system. The work is solid, well-evaluated, and of interest to the NLP community focusing on semantic parsing and feature-based approaches. I recommend acceptance for dissemination (oral presentation recommended), with encouragement to expand discussion of decoding details, error analysis, and reproducibility (hyperparameters and runtime) in a final version or accompanying release.
